name: Cross to Serel
description: General Cross is reaching across the command line to Commander Serel, asking directly whether she's reviewed Karee's data. This is the first official cross-faction communication at command level in three hundred years.
accept_text: He finishes addressing the letter and seals it with the specific formality of someone who understands that the document's form matters as much as its content. 'This communication has the format of a joint coordination request because that's the format the Armistice Accord provides for.' He sets it down. 'Commander Serel is the Terran officer with command authority in the Armistice zone. I have met her twice — once at the original Armistice negotiation, once at the three-year review. She is thorough and she does not waste words.' He looks at the letter. 'She knows about the Grid. She's been at Seraphina Spire. She heard the frequency fifteen years ago and filed it as an anomalous resonance event.' He looks at you with the expression of a commanding officer who has inferred more than was told to him. 'The Skyborn Fleet has been mapping lattice activity for eight months. I don't believe Serel's intelligence apparatus has failed to notice that the Fleet has been mapping something specific in the Wastes corridor.' He picks up the letter. 'I want to know what she's decided at the command level. Not what she's filed. What she's decided.' He hands you the letter. 'You're the carrier. You have access to both sides because you've been moving between them. That's not an accident at this point — you've become the connection the Armistice Accord doesn't formally provide for.' He pauses. 'Tell her the letter is from Cross, that it's formal but the question inside it is genuine, and that I'm asking as one commander to another rather than as opposing parties.'
complete_text: Serel reads the letter standing at her command map, which is where she reads everything. She reads it twice. Then she sets it on the map table, precisely centered, which is a specific gesture of someone who has decided something about a document's status. 'General Cross.' She says it the way she says things she's been expecting. 'The coordination request is in the correct format.' She doesn't pick up the letter again. 'Karee.' She says it differently — quiet, the specific quality of a commander who has been wrong about a subordinate and knows it. 'Karee has been recording the pulse intervals for eight months. She submitted an informal request to include the data in her patrol reports three months ago. I declined it as unsupported observation.' She looks at the letter. 'She didn't stop recording.' She is quiet for a long moment. 'Cross's projection — the node sequence, the timing. He's right about the Armistice corridor.' She looks at the map. 'I've been managing this at the individual command level for eight months because I didn't want either government asking questions about the Grid until we understood what was happening.' She pauses. 'Cross has understood what's happening. He got there through his Fleet observations and a Terran front-line soldier's handwritten timing data.' She is quiet. 'Tell him: yes. Tell him I've reviewed the data — I'm reviewing it now — and I agree on the coordination priority.' She writes something on the back of the letter and hands it back to you. 'And tell him from me, personally, not in the format of the coordination request: I would like to speak with him directly. Neutral ground. Not Armistice ground — the monastery would do.' A pause. 'Tell him I'll make the arrangements if he agrees.'
